title: Pinch-and-coil mug in air-dry clay
subtitle: A handled mug form built for dry use only — pencil cups, utensil holders, or display
excerpt: A mug shape built by combining a pinch-pot body with coiled walls for height, then attaching a pulled coil handle. Air-dry clay is not food-safe and not waterproof, so this form works as a pencil cup, brush holder, utensil caddy, or decorative display piece.
sourceNotes: Combination pinch-and-coil building technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to join and score. The right stage for attaching handles.
Scratching joining surfaces and brushing them with slurry before pressing together.
Unfired clay in any drying stage.

projectSchedule.title: Build the body
projectSchedule.body: Form the base and build the walls up to the full mug height. Let the walls firm to leather-hard before attaching the handle.

projectSchedule.title: Attach the handle
projectSchedule.body: The body should be leather-hard. Attach the handle, support it in position, and let the full form dry slowly for two more days.

content.text: A mug shape built in air-dry clay using pinching for the base and coiling for the walls, with a handle attached at
stage. The result is a convincing mug form, usable as a pencil cup, brush holder, or utensil caddy. Air-dry clay is porous and not food-safe: do not use for drinking or for food storage.

Take 100 g of clay and pinch it into an open bowl form about 8 cm in diameter, walls 6-7 mm thick, height about 4 cm. This is the lower half of the mug. Set it on the board to stiffen slightly for 15-20 minutes uncovered.

Roll two coils of 10 mm diameter. Score the rim of the pinch-pot base and brush with slurry. Add the first coil around the rim and blend the inside seam down. Add the second coil and blend. This brings the mug to about 9-10 cm tall.

Press the rim level with a damp finger and smooth with a wooden rib. The top should be an even circle of consistent wall thickness. Let the body firm to leather-hard before continuing to the handle: a soft body does not hold the handle's weight evenly.
4. Roll and curve a coil handle

Roll a coil 12 mm thick and 12 cm long for the handle. Curve it into an elongated D-shape and rest it on the board for 10-15 minutes so it firms slightly before attachment. A handle attached when too soft will stretch and thin under its own weight once on the mug.

method at both attachment points on the mug body: score and slurry both the mug surface and the handle ends before pressing together. The upper attachment should be 5-10 mm below the rim; the lower attachment about 3 cm above the base. Press firmly and hold for 30 seconds. Smooth the attachment points with a damp finger so the handle blends into the body.

Support the handle while drying with a small wad of plastic or crumpled tissue placed inside the curve so the handle cannot sag. Dry slowly under plastic for the first day, then open for two more days.
air-dry clay is fragile; do not rush the drying.
Once bone-dry, sand rough areas and seal with PVA. Do not use for drinking. The sealed mug holds brushes, pencils, or wooden spoons reliably.

items.symptom: Handle cracks at the attachment points
items.cause: Handle was attached when the body was too dry, or the body and handle were at different moisture stages
items.fix: Rebind with paper clay slurry; cover the whole mug loosely with plastic to equalise moisture

items.cause: Handle was not supported while drying
items.fix: Support the curve from inside with a small wad of tissue or foam

items.symptom: Handle pulls away from the body
items.cause: Score-and-slip was not used before attaching
items.fix: Gently push the detached end back, add fresh slurry, and hold firm for a full minute
